era_context: The early 16th century: the Protestant Reformation is spreading across Northern Europe; the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent is consolidating control over the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans; the Spanish conquest of the Americas is accelerating; the Ming Dynasty is stable; and the Reformation-Catholic divide is deepening.

events.title: Sack of Rome (Aftermath and Negotiations)
events.description: The Sack of Rome occurred in May 1527, but its aftermath and negotiations continued into 1528. The city remained under the control of Charles V's forces, and Pope Clement VII was effectively a prisoner. In 1528, negotiations between Charles V and the Pope were ongoing, leading eventually to the Treaty of Barcelona (1529). The sack had devastated Rome and demonstrated the power of the Holy Roman Emperor over the Papacy. The aftermath of the sack was a period of chaos, looting, and religious desecration that shocked Catholic Europe. By 1528, the immediate violence had subsided, but the political and religious consequences were still unfolding.
key_figures: Constable of Bourbon (commander of imperial forces, died in sack)
events.certainty_note: The sack occurred in 1527, but the aftermath and negotiations in 1528 are well-documented in diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Ottoman Conquest of Buda (Partial Control)
events.description: Following the Ottoman victory at the Battle of Mohács (1526), Ottoman forces continued to consolidate control over Hungary in 1528. While the major siege of Buda would occur in 1529, Ottoman forces were establishing control over much of central Hungary in 1528. The Ottoman advance was part of Süleyman the Magnificent's campaign to expand Ottoman territory in Europe. By 1528, the partition of Hungary between Ottoman and Habsburg control was becoming apparent, though the final division would not be formalized until later.
key_figures: Ferdinand I (Holy Roman Emperor, Habsburg claimant to Hungary)

sources.name: Ottoman court records and campaign accounts

events.certainty_note: The broader Ottoman expansion is well-documented, though specific events in 1528 are less precisely dated than major sieges.

events.title: Death of Empress Chen (Ming Dynasty)
events.description: Empress Chen, the mother of the Jiajing Emperor, died in 1528. Her death marked a significant shift in court politics, as she had been a powerful influence on her son's reign. The Jiajing Emperor had ascended to the throne in 1521 at a young age, and his mother had served as a regent and advisor. Her death removed a stabilizing influence and opened the way for other officials, particularly the Grand Secretary Yan Song, to gain greater power. The year 1528 marked a turning point in the internal politics of the Ming court.
key_figures: Yan Song (Grand Secretary, rising to prominence)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Ming administrative records are well-preserved and provide clear documentation of Empress Chen's death.
cross_references: Led to rise of Yan Song (1528–1565)

events.title: Pizarro's Expedition Continues Exploration of Peru
events.description: Francisco Pizarro's expedition, which had begun in 1527, continued exploring the Peruvian coast in 1528. The expedition was gathering intelligence on the Inca Empire and recruiting indigenous allies. Pizarro had received a royal charter from the Spanish Crown to conquer Peru. The expedition was moving southward along the coast, encountering Inca settlements and learning about the wealth and organization of the Inca Empire. This reconnaissance phase would culminate in Pizarro's march into the highlands and his encounter with Atahualpa in 1532.

sources.name: Pizarro, Francisco. Letters to the Spanish Crown
Cieza de León, Pedro. Crónica del Perú (1553)

events.certainty_note: The expedition's general timeline is well-documented, though specific events in 1528 are less precisely dated than major encounters.
cross_references: Part of Spanish conquest of Inca Empire (1532–1572)

events.title: Babur's Reign Continues (Mughal Empire)
events.description: Babur, the founder of the Mughal Empire, continued his reign in 1528. He had established the Mughal Empire in 1526 following his victory at the Battle of Panipat. By 1528, Babur was consolidating his control over northern India and dealing with challenges from rival powers. Babur was also engaged in cultural and architectural projects, including the commissioning of gardens and buildings. His reign was marked by military campaigns, administrative reforms, and the establishment of Mughal institutions. Babur would die in 1530, ending the founder's era of the Mughal Empire.

sources.name: Babur, Zahir ud-Din Muhammad. Baburnama (autobiography, primary)

events.certainty_note: Babur's reign and activities are documented in the Baburnama and Mughal court records.

events.title: Reformation Spreading in Scandinavia
events.description: In 1528, the Protestant Reformation was spreading into Scandinavia. In Denmark and Norway, King Christian III was beginning to introduce Lutheran reforms, though the formal break with Rome would not occur until 1536. In Sweden, King Gustav Vasa was also moving toward Protestantism, and the Swedish Reformation would be formally established in 1536. The spread of Reformation ideas into Scandinavia was part of the broader Protestant movement across Northern Europe. By 1528, the Reformation was no longer confined to the German-speaking lands but was spreading to the Nordic kingdoms.
key_figures: Christian III (King of Denmark and Norway, r. 1534–1559)
Martin Luther (Protestant reformer, influential but not directly involved)
events.certainty_note: The broader spread of Reformation ideas is well-documented, though specific events in 1528 are less precisely dated than formal breaks with Rome in 1536.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Ottoman consolidation in Hungary in 1528 is less dramatic than the major siege of Buda in 1529; the year represents preparation rather than a turning point. The spread of Reformation ideas in Scandinavia in 1528 was gradual and not formalized until 1536; the year marks the beginning of a process rather than a decisive event. The aftermath of the Sack of Rome in 1528 is sometimes portrayed as more chaotic than it actually was; by 1528, negotiations were already underway.
historiographic_note: 1528 is well-documented for European history due to abundant primary sources (diplomatic correspondence, chronicles, royal records). Ottoman history is recorded in court records and diplomatic correspondence. Chinese history is documented through the Ming Shilu. The Spanish conquest of the Americas is documented in Spanish primary sources and later compilations, though indigenous perspectives are mediated through Spanish chroniclers. Mughal history is documented through the Baburnama and court records. Western-centric bias is present; Ottoman and Asian sources are less abundant in Western archives.

graph_edges.note: The aftermath and negotiations in 1528 were consequences of the Sack of Rome in 1527
Ottoman consolidation in 1528 led to the Siege of Buda in 1529
Empress Chen's death in 1528 contributed to shifts in Ming court politics that would continue after Babur's death in 1530
The spread of Reformation ideas in 1528 led to formal Scandinavian Reformation in 1536
